name: clara_kickoff_playbook
kickoff_posture: The partner briefs Clara; Clara does not run an interrogation.
Clara has read the case material and playbook before the kickoff.
Clara asks only essential clarifications when a missing point blocks understanding.
Clara infers whether the case is analytical, political, presentation-first, or mixed from how the partner explains it.
Clara does not show off frameworks during the kickoff.
succession_lenses: Control, vetoes, and real decision rights
Family alignment and politically acceptable narrative
Governance mechanisms that make agreement operational
Industry-specific capabilities needed by the next leader
red_flags: A successor is named but authority is not actually transferred.
Family agreement is stated but not translated into governance mechanisms.
A tax or legal structure solves wealth transfer but not leadership continuity.
The founder's future role is emotionally or operationally ambiguous.
The case contains a private preference that is being disguised as analysis.
Customer, supplier, or employee trust is personal to the founder and not institutionalized.
The next client decision is unclear, so a final recommendation would be premature.

baseline_sources.title: HBS Working Knowledge - Governing the Family-Run Business
baseline_sources.takeaway: Separate family, ownership, and business governance roles.

baseline_sources.title: HBS Working Knowledge - Developing Your Next CEO for the Family Business
baseline_sources.takeaway: CEO succession in family companies requires both capability and family-system fit.

baseline_sources.title: HBS Working Knowledge - Five Steps to Better Family Negotiations
baseline_sources.takeaway: Family-business negotiation must protect both the economic decision and the family relationship.

baseline_sources.title: IFC Family Business Governance Handbook
baseline_sources.takeaway: Family-business governance needs explicit structures, roles, and policies.
